21.correct: The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of India is the constitutional authority (Article 148) that audits the accounts of the Union and the state governments.

wrong.A: The Finance Commission recommends tax sharing, it does not audit accounts.
wrong.C: NITI Aayog is a policy think-tank, not an auditor.
wrong.D: The UPSC conducts recruitment exams for the civil services.

21.fact: The CAG is often called the guardian of the public purse; its reports are examined by the Public Accounts Committee.

22.correct: A Financial Emergency (Article 360) has never been proclaimed in India.

wrong.B: President's Rule (Article 356) has been used many times in the states.
wrong.D: 'None of the above' is wrong because the Financial Emergency is the one never used.

22.fact: The closest India came to a financial crisis without Article 360 was the 1991 balance-of-payments crisis.

23.correct: The Governor appoints the Advocate General of a State (Article 165), the highest law officer of the state.

wrong.A: The Chief Minister advises but does not formally appoint the Advocate General.
wrong.B: The State Legislature has no appointing power here.
wrong.D: The President appoints the Attorney General for the Union, not the state's Advocate General.

23.fact: The Advocate General is the state-level equivalent of the Attorney General of India.

24.correct: The human ear can hear sound in the frequency range of about 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz).

wrong.A: 2-200 Hz is far below the full audible range.
wrong.C: 200-2000 Hz is only a part of the audible range.
wrong.D: 2000-20,000 Hz covers only the higher part of the range.

24.fact: Sound below 20 Hz is called infrasound and above 20 kHz is ultrasound.

25.correct: 'Hyperion' was written by John Keats, not William Wordsworth, so it is the odd one out.

wrong.A: 'The Excursion' is a long poem by Wordsworth.
wrong.B: 'Intimations of Immortality' is a famous ode by Wordsworth.
wrong.D: 'Michael' is a pastoral poem by Wordsworth.

25.fact: Wordsworth, with Coleridge, launched English Romantic poetry with 'Lyrical Ballads' (1798).

26.correct: Urban PM2.5 in India comes from several major sources together - industrial emissions, construction dust and vehicle exhaust - so 'all of these' is correct.

wrong.A: Industrial emissions are one source but not the only one.
wrong.B: Construction dust is significant but not the sole contributor.
wrong.C: Vehicle exhaust is major but combined with the others.

26.fact: PM2.5 refers to fine particles under 2.5 micrometres that penetrate deep into the lungs.

27.correct: Rain temporarily improves air quality (lowers AQI) by washing pollutants and dust out of the air.

wrong.A: High humidity without rain can trap pollutants and worsen AQI.
wrong.B: Calm wind allows pollutants to accumulate, raising AQI.
wrong.C: Temperature inversion traps pollutants near the ground, worsening AQI.

27.fact: A higher AQI value means worse air; rain causes 'wet deposition' of particulate matter.

28.correct: Neeraj Chopra won his first Asian Games javelin gold in 2018 at Jakarta.

wrong.A: 2014 Incheon - Neeraj had not yet emerged at the senior level.
wrong.C: 2022 Hangzhou - he won gold again, but not his first.
wrong.D: 2010 Guangzhou - too early in his career.

28.fact: Neeraj Chopra has won Olympic gold (2020), World Championship gold (2023) and multiple Asian/Commonwealth titles.

29.correct: Athletes who misuse anabolic steroids do so primarily to build muscle mass and strength.

wrong.A: Increasing endurance is more associated with blood-boosting agents like EPO.
wrong.B: Steroids do not specifically enhance flexibility.
wrong.D: Reducing fatigue is associated with stimulants, not anabolic steroids.

29.fact: Anabolic steroids are banned in sport and are on the WADA prohibited list.

30.correct: The thyroid gland requires iodine to produce the hormone thyroxine (T4).

wrong.A: Calcium is needed for bones and is regulated by other hormones.
wrong.C: Sodium is an electrolyte, not the element in thyroxine.
wrong.D: Potassium is an electrolyte, not used to make thyroxine.

30.fact: Iodine deficiency causes goitre; iodised salt is used to prevent it.

31.correct: The Goods and Services Tax (GST) Council is chaired by the Union Finance Minister of India.

wrong.A: The Prime Minister does not chair the GST Council.
wrong.B: The NITI Aayog CEO is not the chairperson.
wrong.C: The RBI Governor handles monetary policy, not the GST Council.

31.fact: State finance ministers are members of the GST Council, making it a federal body (Article 279A).

32.correct: Aurangzeb's long Deccan campaigns were mainly aimed at subduing the Marathas, who waged persistent guerrilla resistance.

wrong.A: He fought the Rajputs earlier, but the Deccan wars targeted the Marathas.
wrong.C: The Afghans were a frontier concern, not the focus of the Deccan campaigns.
wrong.D: The Portuguese held coastal pockets but were not the main target.

32.fact: The exhausting Deccan wars drained the Mughal treasury and contributed to the empire's decline.

33.correct: The PM-DAKSH scheme targets both Scheduled Castes (I) and Other Backward Classes (II), among other marginalised groups, so 'Both I and II' is correct.

wrong.A: 'Only I' leaves out OBCs, who are also covered.
wrong.B: 'Only II' leaves out SCs, who are also covered.
wrong.D: 'Neither' is wrong since both groups are target beneficiaries.

33.fact: PM-DAKSH also covers EWS, safai karamcharis and de-notified tribes for free skill training.

34.correct: The Pallava king Narasimhavarman I defeated and killed the Chalukya ruler Pulakesin II (around 642 CE) and captured the Chalukya capital Vatapi.

wrong.B: Parantaka I was a Chola king.
wrong.D: Amoghavarsha I was a Rashtrakuta king.

34.fact: For this victory Narasimhavarman I took the title 'Vatapikonda' (conqueror of Vatapi).

35.correct: The 'Kambala' festival - a traditional buffalo race held in slushy paddy fields - is celebrated in Karnataka (coastal/Tulu Nadu region).

wrong.B: Gujarat is not associated with Kambala.

35.fact: Kambala has gained fame after some runners clocked sprint times comparable to professional athletes.

36.correct: The 3-day International Bird Festival 2025 was organised at Prayagraj (Uttar Pradesh) from 16 to 18 February 2025.

wrong.B: Bhopal was not the host city.
wrong.C: Patna did not host this festival.

36.fact: Such bird festivals promote birdwatching and wetland/biodiversity conservation.

37.correct: Sri Aurobindo Ghose delivered a speech in Bombay on 15 January 1908 stressing that national education should awaken the spirit of nationalism in students.

wrong.A: Rabindranath Tagore promoted education (Santiniketan) but did not give this Bombay speech.
wrong.C: Lala Lajpat Rai was an extremist leader but not the speaker here.
wrong.D: Mahatma Gandhi was still in South Africa in 1908.

37.fact: Sri Aurobindo was a leading figure of the Swadeshi and national-education movements before turning to spirituality.

38.correct: Between the 2001 and 2011 censuses, the Muslim community recorded the highest decadal population growth rate among India's religious groups.

wrong.A: The Sikh community had a lower growth rate.
wrong.C: The Buddhist community grew more slowly.
wrong.D: The Hindu community grew more slowly than the Muslim community.

38.fact: The overall national population growth rate has been declining decade on decade across communities.

39.correct: Both borrowings (I) and taxation (II) are legitimate ways by which a government finances its expenditure and bridges a budgetary gap, so neither is excluded - hence 'Neither I nor II'.

wrong.A: 'Only I' wrongly excludes taxation as a source of finance.
wrong.C: 'Both I and II' would mean both are NOT ways, which is incorrect.

39.fact: A government typically meets its spending through tax revenue, non-tax revenue and borrowings.

40.correct: Operation 'Gideon's Chariots' is associated with Israel - it was the name of an Israeli military operation in Gaza in 2025.

wrong.A: India is not associated with this operation.
wrong.C: Turkey is not linked to it.
wrong.D: The UAE is not associated with the operation.

40.fact: Military operations are often given codenames drawn from history or mythology.
